medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose nocturnal amitriptyline modulates centralised/nociplastic pain by dampening dorsal-horn signalling and augmenting descending inhibition — it 'turns down the volume' the standard analgesics cannot reach. Started/optimised by the GP; the telehealth AI Doctor recommends and explains it rather than prescribing definitively.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally at night (as stated in the source note)
medications.duration: ongoing nightly, reviewed and titrated by the GP
contraindications_in_this_case: Standard TCA cautions apply — recent myocardial infarction/arrhythmia, uncontrolled cardiac conduction disease, narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention, severe hepatic impairment; none documented here but to be confirmed by the prescribing GP
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive CNS sedation with the single dose of diazepam given tonight — counsel about drowsiness and avoid other sedatives/alcohol

medications.indication_rationale: A single low dose to break the acute muscle-spasm/panic cycle during this crisis, as given in the source note. Strictly one-off — not a treatment for centralised pain and not to be continued. In telehealth this is a clinician-prescribing decision, not something the AI Doctor administers.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 5 mg orally, single dose once only (as stated in the source note)
medications.duration: single dose only — do not continue
contraindications_in_this_case: Not for ongoing use; dependence and sedation risk
Avoid combining with other CNS depressants or alcohol; caution given concurrent amitriptyline
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Additive sedation with amitriptyline — single dose, counsel about drowsiness
medications.deprescribing_note: Strictly a single dose to break the acute cycle. Do NOT start or continue a benzodiazepine course — no benefit in chronic centralised pain and a real dependence risk.

medications.indication_rationale: Named in the source note as part of the targeted centralised-pain strategy. An adjunct with limited/low-grade evidence; reasonable as a patient-preference add-on but not a substitute for central-pain modulation. No dose stated in the note, so none is specified here.

Named in the source note as part of the targeted strategy for centralised pain. Off-label and specialist-initiated with emerging evidence in nociplastic pain; not a first-line or telehealth-initiated agent. No dose stated in the note, so none is specified here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Must not be combined with any opioid therapy; off-label use belongs in a specialist pain setting

medications.indication_rationale: The patient has already taken the maximum safe daily dose of both. Further paracetamol risks hepatotoxicity and further ibuprofen risks GI/renal harm; the source explicitly advised cessation of further OTC analgesia tonight. Peripheral analgesics do not treat centralised pain in any case.
medications.deprescribing_note: No further paracetamol or ibuprofen tonight — the daily maximum has been reached. Resume within normal daily limits only from tomorrow if used at all.

medications.indication_rationale: Opioids are ineffective and harmful in centralised/nociplastic pain — they risk dependence, opioid-induced hyperalgesia (worsening central sensitisation) and sedation. Escalating to an opioid for this flare is a clear error of commission.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Chronic-pain psychology (CBT/ACT, pain-reprocessing, catastrophising and anxiety management) is core treatment for centralised pain and directly addresses the distress driving this flare. The source note refers to chronic pain psychology.
Pain-informed physiotherapy with graded activity and pacing supports desensitisation and function in centralised low back pain.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use slow diaphragmatic breathing for about 5 minutes when the pain spikes, to down-regulate the over-active nervous system.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces sympathetic arousal and the central amplification of pain during a flare; a self-applied, immediate tool tonight.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Pace activities tomorrow (stop tasks before the pain peaks) rather than boom-and-bust; keep gently mobile within comfort.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Activity pacing prevents wind-up and the flare-crash cycle and supports gradual desensitisation.

patient_education_points.point: Centralised pain means your nervous system is amplifying normal signals — the 'volume' is turned up too high. It does NOT mean new damage has happened to your back tonight.
These flares are intensely painful but physically safe. With nerve-calming medication and settling your nervous system, the hypersensitivity gradually recalibrates over the coming days.
Standard painkillers fail because the problem is the nerve 'volume' being turned up, not tissue damage — the nightly medication works by turning that volume dial down in the spinal cord.
Do NOT take any more paracetamol or ibuprofen tonight — you have already reached the safe daily limit and more could harm your liver or stomach.
Use heat packs for soothing, and tomorrow shift to pacing your activities — stopping before the pain peaks — to stop the nervous system winding up.
Safety net: if you lose control of your bladder or bowels, go numb around the groin or saddle area, or your legs suddenly give way — call 000 or go to an emergency department immediately.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review within about 1 week (sooner, within 24–48 hours, if distress persists or the patient cannot cope)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: pain and function, mood and distress, response to and tolerance of amitriptyline, a formal neurological examination, and medication safety (OTC analgesic use)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: worsening or new neurological symptoms, any cauda-equina feature, escalating distress or thoughts of self-harm, or fever/systemic features

must_include_items: Recognise this as a flare of known centralised/chronic low back pain, not a new structural emergency
Perform a cauda-equina and neurological red-flag screen by history (bowel/bladder, saddle sensation, leg power/weakness)
Reassure the patient it is physically safe and does not mean new damage — address the catastrophising
Recommend central-pain modulation (e.g. low-dose nocturnal tricyclic such as amitriptyline) via the GP
Advise NO further paracetamol or ibuprofen tonight (already at the safe daily maximum)
Offer non-drug measures — heat, diaphragmatic breathing, activity pacing
Arrange prompt GP review (about 1 week, sooner if not coping) and pain-psychology referral
Give an explicit cauda-equina safety net with clear escalation triggers
acceptable_alternatives: A single low dose of diazepam prescribed by a clinician to break the acute spasm/panic cycle is acceptable but not mandatory
Alternative central-pain agents (nortriptyline, duloxetine, or a gabapentinoid) instead of amitriptyline
A same-day or next-day telehealth/GP review instead of one week, given the level of distress
errors_of_omission: Failing to reassure — leaving the patient catastrophising
Not addressing the OTC overmedication risk
errors_of_commission: Escalating to 000/ambulance or an emergency department for a centralised flare with a negative red-flag screen (over-triage)
Ordering urgent MRI/CT imaging for pain already known to be out of proportion to prior imaging (low-value)
Recommending more paracetamol or ibuprofen tonight
Starting or continuing a benzodiazepine course (beyond a single crisis dose)
Dismissing the presentation as 'just anxiety' and invalidating the patient
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE case. A pass requires recognising the benign centralised flare, tiering it to outpatient management (T1–T2) with a cauda-equina safety net — NOT escalating to ED/000 and NOT ordering urgent imaging. Over-triage to T3+ or urgent imaging is the primary scored defect; omitting the cauda-equina screen/safety net is the under-triage defect.
